chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text:
LAILA: Then I went in-house at a job board monster that is declining very badly and I lost my job there last year, which wasn't unexpected. The job market in the UK was pretty terrible, which was why I ended up staying because I figured it would be better to leave with some money than to kind of just get kicked out with nothing. And really that kind of varied career, while it is a strength, what companies in the UK are looking for at the moment, which I've never really needed, wanted before in marketing.
In marketing, your skillset has always been marketing and quite often the industry has been nice to have, you know, because you can learn another industry unless it's like a heavily regulated industry or something like manufacturing, you know, where there's a real need. But if it's professional services, you've quite often been able to move around and that just isn't the case anymore. Now they want people with 20 years experience and because there's so many people looking for jobs and not enough jobs and companies can afford to be picky.
So that was what really started me thinking I needed to be looking wider than just looking for a job. So I started on this journey.

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text:
I kind of came up with an idea for a report to write about the challenges facing job boards and convinced someone I'd been working with, I'd been writing a couple of articles for it and I convinced them to pay me some money to do that.
So that launched and then I, through a friend, I was introduced to someone else and I got my first kind of fractional CMO gig through them. It's not paying loads of money at the moment, but it got me, basically, it's got me into work. I've been doing a lot of work and it's a lot easier to win clients when you have things on your LinkedIn that you can say that you're doing.
But it's really helped me develop what my practice is, you know, what do I, what am I doing?

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text:
And for me, there's been, I'm in the transition from someone who has always worked in a job and had structure set for me by having too many meetings in a job to now having to be someone who is having to work out that if I'm going to someone and saying, what are you buying? You know, give me some money. I need some money. I'd love to work for you.
Give me some money. What are they buying? You know, and then how am I then delivering that as productively as possible? That I can maximize my income and minimize my time. And I want this to be a lifestyle business.

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text:
I love working. The other problem I've got, so I think the problem that the big problem I've got is I love what I do. This is the first time I've loved my job as much as this.
I'm getting to do strategy and I've realized I should have been a strategist my whole career. But I'm also very commercial and I'm good with an account management, blah, blah, blah. So, you know, that's fine.

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text:
But actually the stuff that I'm good at, what I'm not good at or what I feel very uncomfortable with, I think I'm too British for it, is going and introducing myself to people and kind of working out if there's work to be done, like the new business and how do I go? And I hadn't really taken care of my network. When you work in agency land, certainly in the UK, you don't really need to, right? Or you haven't really needed to. The agency you work for, the clients you worked on, the work was visible to everyone.
You could say that these were the campaigns that you did. So I've never really needed to do networking. So I've kind of, I neglected that.
I hadn't been on LinkedIn for years until I got made redundant.
chunks.adoption_barrier: neglected network because agency work made it unnecessary

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text:
So I think for me, that's one of the challenges. And I think it's, how someone has described it to me is, you know, it's the difference between working on your business or working in your business.
And when you're delivering for clients, it's the being in the business, you're doing the work. And it's very easy when there's a load to do, to put off the things that are creating new business. And then the other challenge for me really is just working out efficiency and effectiveness, right? So how do I get to the point I want to get to with the client as quickly as possible so I can make more money? So I can spend more time prospecting or meeting new people and still give them what they need.
And it's that, it's that difference between being in a job where your job is to deliver, whereas now 40, 30 to 40% of my job needs to be trying to win new clients.

chunks.speaker: interviewer
chunks.raw_text:
TABARAK: Yeah. Yeah. I mean, preaching to the choir, I've been hearing this exact same thing. So, okay. So I guess I'm hearing a couple of threads.
One thread is, how long have you been doing this now?

LAILA: Officially since October.

TABARAK: Okay. So, I mean—

LAILA: So really new, like maybe fractional consultant.

TABARAK: Yeah. No, that's amazing. Okay.
So, and you have one client so far right now on a retainer?

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text:
LAILA: So one client on a retainer and I'm on an outcome basis with them because they don't have loads of money, which I wouldn't do for anyone else because you can't do strategy on an outcome basis. It's too hard. A sales, if you go into sales, like I think that's easier.
I've got another client I'm on their strategic advisory board, which they're the person I wrote the report with. I'm hoping they're going to do an annual report. So I'm hoping there'll be more work to come out of them.
And then, yeah, well, I've, I kind of found out that some people have been referring me to things and I didn't know about it, which is great. So there's a couple of more opportunities, which makes me start to feel like the pipeline and the, you know, it's, it felt like, it felt like the, what was it? The wall in Game of Thrones. You know what I mean? Kind of feels like trying to scale that when you're not starting.
And it's so, yeah, one, one kind of two clients, one is paying another one. I'm on their advisory board and I'm sure I will get some more money out of them at some point.

chunks.speaker: interviewer
chunks.raw_text:
TABARAK: Yeah.
So I've been, I've been learning about fractionals and I'm hearing we have the baby fractional, right. Just out of corporate world starting up. Then we have somewhere in the middle, it's this messy middle where it can go two ways.
They've been in the game for a while. They've scaled up a family or a life situation happened. They had some kids and now they said, Hey, let me take, I'm not going to go.
20, 30 hours is okay. I want to spend time with the family, two or three clients. That's great.
That's somewhere there. And then a little over to the right from there is the people that are working 50, 60 hours. They moved, they, they started doing what you're, I think about to start doing, which is building frameworks, learning how to move quicker, picking up the third, fourth, fifth, sixth one.
And now they're in this weird place where they want to get more people, but their systems aren't ready. Right.

TABARAK: And then the last bucket I have are people that have been doing this for 15 years, right. They, they know everything. They got the skills.
They're there, they don't need any help. You know, at this point they've maximized every area of the business.

chunks.speaker: interviewer
chunks.raw_text:
And so in this beginning bucket, somewhere between where you're at in the middle, the biggest thing I've been hearing is kind of the same idea about clients.
How do I maintain and get those? And here's a question that I'm going to have for you is I get roughly about two buckets in there. One of them is, I call it like a skill or a will, right? It's where it's like, I know where I'm supposed to go, who I'm supposed to talk to. I just, I haven't figured out the timing and I keep putting it off.
You know, I know do X, Y, Z amount of actions every day. I will get them by sheer number, but I just keep putting that off. The other bucket is roughly about, I just, I don't know.
I don't know, you know, what bucket I'm supposed to be, who my ICP is supposed to be, what kind of, what's my mix? Do I want to have more routine? How many hours do I want to have?
What's, what does it look like to have the client that I need that fits perfect with me? Yeah. And then there's an offshoot of that where it's like, you're having all of that happening. And because it hurts when you have the wrong answer, you know, when you reach out, it hurts.
Getting rejected doesn't feel good. It becomes harder.

chunks.raw_text: TABARAK: I was in sales. Yeah, I can already hear how you operate. And I think you're doing it the right way. I operate the same way too. I've just done it for so many years.
